responses.strategic_rationale: This residential parasomnia research unit navigates three fundamental tensions: safety versus naturalism (clinical oversight must not suppress the target behavior), operational scalability versus phenotypic yield (larger cohorts demand proportional staffing and event-capture risk), and data richness versus annotation burden (multi-modal sensing generates complexity that threatens publication velocity). The chosen levers address recruitment fidelity and event-capture rate—the binding constraints on scientific validity—alongside cost containment and publication timeline, because enrollment shortfalls or event droughts trigger external review at month 18 regardless of operational excellence.

levers.name: Participant eligibility and event-yield targeting
levers.consequences: Strict pre-admission screening (specialist interview, polysomnography, bed-partner corroboration, epilepsy exclusion) reduces recruitment speed but increases the probability that admitted participants will capture events during their stay, directly improving the event-yield floor. Conversely, relaxing screening to accelerate enrollment risks admitting participants with mimics or low intrinsic event frequency, which erodes the 70–80% capture assumption and can exhaust extension stays without publishable data.
options: Implement a two-stage telephone-plus-in-person screening protocol with a board-certified neurologist reviewing all exclusion-checklist edge cases before enrollment, accepting a 6–8 week pre-admission pipeline to ensure only high-yield, low-risk participants occupy bed days.

Delegate initial screening to referring sleep physicians using a structured checklist and automated decision-tree tool, fast-tracking enrollment within 2–3 weeks at the cost of accepting occasional mis-screened admissions that do not capture events.

Recruit a subset of participants (25% of cohort) from inpatient epilepsy monitoring units where recent EEG and seizure-disorder status are already established, bypassing external screening overhead but requiring formal data-sharing agreements with hospital partners.
levers.review_lever: Stricter screening delays recruitment but concentrates event yield; relaxed screening accelerates numbers but risks unproductive admissions. None of the three options directly address the 20% unproductive-admission ceiling trigger mechanism—what happens if month 18 review finds 22% of admissions captured zero events despite meeting entry criteria?

levers.name: Stay duration and extension policy
levers.consequences: Baseline 2–8 week stays allow time for infrequent events to manifest, but participants with zero events after 8 weeks can extend up to 10 weeks total, consuming bed capacity and staff annotation time without scientific return. Shorter baseline stays (e.g., 4 weeks) reduce per-participant resource consumption but compress event windows and increase the fraction reaching the extension cap, while longer baseline stays (e.g., 10 weeks) front-load costs but may front-load yields for low-frequency phenotypes.
options: Fix the baseline stay at 4 weeks with a single 2-week extension option only for participants with documented pre-admission events at ≥1 per week, capping extension time at 6 weeks total and prioritizing bed turnover for fresh recruitment.

Stratify stays based on pre-admission event frequency (self-reported ≥1 per week: 4-week baseline; 2–4 per month: 6-week baseline; < 2 per month: 8-week baseline), with conditional 2-week extensions only if no events are captured by week 6, aligning stay length to expected yield.

Offer uniform 8-week stays to all participants and eliminate extensions entirely, accepting slower enrollment but ensuring comparable exposure duration for between-person phenotyping and reducing decision fatigue around extension adjudication.
levers.review_lever: Shorter stays preserve bed throughput but lose rare long-interval events; longer stays burden staffing and may trap resources in low-yield cases. All three options assume pre-admission frequency reports are reliable—none address the participant recall bias or partner-history gaps that could invalidate event-yield predictions.

levers.name: Enhanced-night PSG scheduling frequency
levers.consequences: Current protocol deploys full PSG (scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, leg EMG) on first night, mid-stay, and final night, plus escalation after technician-flagged events, balancing physiological detail against cost and annotation labor. More frequent enhanced nights (e.g., every 3 nights) increase the likelihood of capturing parasomnia morphology in clinical-grade detail but multiply scoring hours and strain the independent-rater bottleneck, risking publication delays. Fewer enhanced nights (e.g., first night only, plus escalation) reduce costs but truncate longitudinal within-person characterization across the Aim 2 phenotyping mandate.
options: Deploy full PSG on the first night, then twice weekly for the remainder of the stay (every 3–4 days), with technician-triggered escalation nights added ad hoc, front-loading detailed phenotyping cost but providing sufficient longitudinal snapshots for within-person clustering and morphology description.

Maintain the current three-fixed-night schedule plus technician-triggered escalations, adding a fourth ad hoc enhanced night only if the participant captures an event during low-burden monitoring but the event morphology is ambiguous on sensor playback, minimizing routine scoring burden.

Use enhanced PSG only on the first night and final night, plus all technician-triggered escalations, eliminating mid-stay baseline monitoring and relying on escalation nights to characterize longitudinal drift, accepting reduced statistical power for within-person temporal clustering but reducing annotation throughput by approximately 30%.
levers.review_lever: More frequent PSG captures phenotypic detail but balloons scoring burden and delays publications; fewer nights preserve speed but weaken within-person longitudinal claims. None of the options address inter-rater disagreement on novel parasomnia morphologies—how are novel phenotypes adjudicated when two scorers disagree on a low-burden event that was never captured on PSG?

levers.name: Semi-automated event-triage development scope and timeline
levers.consequences: Aim 3 mandates benchmarking an event-triage tool against dual human scorer agreement, reducing manual review burden by ranking probable parasomnia events. Aggressive timeline (algorithm development in parallel with data collection, months 6–18) accelerates tool validation but risks training on small early datasets that may not generalize across the full participant mix. Deferred development (algorithm starts at month 12, benchmarked by month 30) allows larger training data and tighter generalization but compresses the final year for iteration and delays operational deployment, potentially forcing manual review of the entire final cohort.
options: Initiate algorithm development at month 6 using data from the first 15–20 enrolled participants, with monthly retraining cycles as new data accumulates, accepting early-stage generalization risk in exchange for operational tool deployment by month 24 to support annotation of late-cohort events.

Defer algorithm development until month 12 when 25–30 participants have enrolled and 60+ adjudicated events are accumulated, then train a single model by month 18 for benchmarking by month 24, reducing iteration overhead but compressing refinement and limiting deployment to retrospective archive analysis.

Outsource semi-automated triage to a specialized computational neuroscience service provider (e.g., a university-affiliated biomedical informatics center) at months 8–20, paying per-algorithm-refinement iteration, offloading internal computational burden but introducing contractual dependency and longer decision-cycle times.
levers.review_lever: Early training risks overfitting to skewed initial data; late training sacrifices operational deployment window. None of the three options specify the false-negative tolerance (missing parasomnia events) versus false-positive tolerance (over-flagging benign arousals)—a critical trade-off that will drive tool adoption by the night technicians.

levers.name: Data sharing, repository choice, and derivative-access policy
levers.consequences: Current plan deposits de-identified physiological data in a recognized repository under data use agreements while restricting bedroom video to on-site access only, balancing open science against privacy constraints and regulatory burden. Open, unrestricted sharing would maximize community access and citation impact but risks privacy-law violations under GDPR and erodes participant confidence in residential monitoring. Closed, institutional-only access preserves privacy but forgoes secondary-research momentum and reuse, limiting the scientific footprint of the 50–70 participant cohort.
options: Deposit de-identified physiological data and event-triage outputs in an international open repository (e.g., OpenNeuro, PhysioNet) with BIDS-standard formatting and minimal data-use-agreement friction, reserving video for institutional-only access through a secure institutional hub; advertise the resource in sleep medicine methods forums to seed secondary-use projects.

Establish a managed-access model via a dedicated institutional repository (University of Bonn's data portal) where external researchers request access via a lightweight application, with data use agreements limited to non-commercial academic use, enabling tracking of reuse while preserving tighter governance than open repositories.

Restrict all physiological data sharing to co-authored collaborative projects initiated by the core team within 3 years post-publication, allowing private-sector partnerships and international collaborations only under formal data-transfer agreements, maximizing control but minimizing secondary-research yield and community benefit.
levers.review_lever: Open sharing maximizes impact but multiplies privacy-review and GDPR-compliance labor; restricted sharing protects privacy but sacrifices scientific reuse and leverage. None of the options explicitly reconcile the tension between open-science funding mandates (increasingly required by DFG grants) and the video-capture sensitivities that make this dataset irreducibly sensitive.

levers.name: Staffing model and shift coverage for night technician roles
levers.consequences: Current plan deploys 3 rotating technicians to cover 8 active suites, providing adequate event-response time and real-time annotation but consuming roughly 60% of personnel budget. Increasing staffing (e.g., 4–5 technicians) improves safety margins and annotation quality but accelerates the cost trajectory and compresses the physical capacity available before a bed-expansion trigger is warranted. Reducing staffing (e.g., 2 technicians with remote backup or daytime-shift annotation catch-up) lowers costs but risks delayed event responses during unusual events and shifts annotation into daytime, potentially introducing scoring fatigue and batch-processing errors.
options: Hire a dedicated night-shift coordinator (in addition to the 3 rotating technicians) to handle real-time event verification, participant welfare checks, and escalation decisions, freeing the technicians for focused annotation and sleep quality, accepting a 15% increase in night-shift personnel cost but improving response times and reducing individual technician cognitive load.

Maintain 3 rotating technicians but introduce a hybrid staffing model where two technicians work the night shift (10pm–6am) for event response and participant safety, and a daytime annotation technician processes flagged-event video during business hours using time-locked physiological playback, reducing night-shift fatigue-related errors.

Reduce staffing to 2 full-time night technicians with an on-call backup technician paid hourly for events flagged as high-probability parasomnia, supplementing night coverage during high-event periods (e.g., weeks 2–4 of stays when event rates often peak), reducing baseline costs while preserving surge capacity.
levers.review_lever: Higher staffing improves response and annotation quality but strains budget; lower staffing cuts costs but risks delayed responses and batch-annotation errors. None of the options address the quality of night-technician decision-making itself—no training or competency assessment schedule is specified for consistent event flagging.

levers.name: Expansion timing and bed-capacity utilization policy
levers.consequences: Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but the plan defers expansion until pilot data (8 suites, months 0–12) demonstrates acceptable data quality, manageable false-alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Early expansion (adding 4 suites by month 8) would accelerate enrollment toward the 50–70 target and reduce per-participant facility overhead, but risks discovering bottlenecks (e.g., annotation backlogs, technician fatigue) only after sunk capital. Delayed expansion (month 18 or later, conditional on metrics) preserves capital flexibility and de-risks staffing scaling, but pushes full-capacity operations into years 2–3 and compresses time for failure-trigger resolution (the month 18 external review threshold).
options: Add 4 suites in parallel with the first 12 months of 8-suite operation, hiring and training additional technicians by month 6 to ensure overlap training with current staff before month 8 activation, accepting earlier capital commitment but shortening the path to 50+ enrollments and spreading setup overhead across a larger bed base.

Conduct a formal readiness review at month 10 (after 8–12 weeks of stable 8-suite operations) assessing data-quality metrics, false-alarm rates, and technician workload; if all metrics are within pre-specified targets, activate 4 additional suites by month 12 with a 4-week technician overlap and training window, deferring major capital until operational proof.

Commit to expand to 12 suites only after the month 18 external review if the program meets enrollment and event-capture benchmarks, conditioning bed expansion on demonstrated operational sustainability and using the interval to refine protocols, reducing early capital risk but squeezing the final 18 months to hit 50–70 enrollments and triggering acceleration pressure.
levers.review_lever: Early expansion scales enrollment faster but risks capital waste and staffing chaos if pilot metrics fail; late expansion preserves flexibility but forces rapid scaling in the final year if month 18 review is positive. All three options assume that adding 4 suites simply scales current staffing ratios linearly—none address the fixed-overhead tail (data engineering, coordination, clinical oversight) that does not scale proportionally with beds.

responses.strategic_rationale: This residential parasomnia unit must navigate three core tensions: (1) naturalistic behavior capture versus clinical safety—too sterile an environment suppresses the spontaneous parasomnia events that justify the expense, yet inadequate safety infrastructure creates liability and staff burnout; (2) event rarity versus operational cost—most nights yield no events, forcing a trade-off between extended participant stays (which stretch the budget and recruitment timeline) and high false-alarm rates from sensors; (3) human annotation bottleneck versus algorithmic risk—manual scoring by trained raters ensures methodological rigor but cannot scale beyond ~50–70 participants before staffing becomes unaffordable, yet premature automation risks publishing flawed phenotypes. The levers below target these tensions by controlling event-yield sensitivity, stay length economics, venue atmosphere, technician workload distribution, and external validation strategy.

levers.name: Venue atmosphere and acoustic design trade-off
levers.consequences: A fully soundproofed, clinically appointed suite maximizes technical data isolation but increases perceived surveillance and suppresses naturalistic parasomnia behavior; conversely, preserving domestic acoustics and furnishings encourages behavioral authenticity but introduces environmental noise that degrades signal-to-noise ratio on dry-electrode EEG and complicates technician alertness to genuine events. The balance directly determines whether captured events are representative of the participant's at-home parasomnia phenotype or an artifact of the research environment.
options: Designate two suites as bedroom-faithful (residential wallpaper, conventional lighting, household noise tolerance) for baseline behavioral collection while maintaining full clinical soundproofing and shielding in the remaining six suites for high-fidelity PSG nights, allowing direct phenotypic comparison between naturalistic and controlled settings.

Install modular acoustic panels and dimmable lighting in all eight suites that can be activated only during the three scheduled enhanced-night PSG sessions, keeping the environment domestic by default and minimizing the clinical atmosphere that might suppress parasomnia expression.

Construct a single hybrid demonstration suite designed with interior surfaces that appear residential but incorporate concealed acoustic isolation and electromagnetic shielding, then rotate all participants through this single suite for their enhanced-night PSG acquisition while conducting low-burden monitoring in the remaining seven standard-residential suites.
levers.review_lever: Domestic feel suppresses event capture, while clinical control degrades phenotypic relevance; the options do not address how to measure whether behavior changes between domestic and clinical sub-settings, leaving inter-setting reproducibility unclear.

levers.name: Participant baseline phenotyping depth and recruitment gatekeeping
levers.consequences: Requiring in-depth pre-admission assessment (specialist interview, collateral history, baseline PSG, exclusion adjudication) ensures enrolled participants meet strict parasomnia criteria and reduces unproductive admissions, but extending the enrollment pathway delays first participant arrival and narrows the recruitment funnel, slowing enrollment velocity and increasing per-participant screening costs. Light gatekeeping (self-report plus single clinical phone screen) accelerates enrollment and broadens the recruitment pool but risks admitting borderline candidates whose events are infrequent, misdiagnosed, or not parasomnia-related, inflating unproductive admissions and depleting the contingency budget.
options: Implement a two-stage intake: tier-one screening by sleep clinic telephone interview plus review of any prior PSG to rapidly filter obvious exclusions, with full adjudication and collateral-history collection reserved for tier-one passers, concentrating gatekeeping effort on the 40–50% of applicants who clear the phone screen.

Conduct baseline polysomnography on all applicants at the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic (leveraging existing infrastructure and clinical-care referrals) before enrollment decision, ensuring parasomnia diagnosis is neurophysiologically confirmed and obstructive sleep apnea severity is measured, accepting the 6–8 week pre-enrollment delay but eliminating on-site unproductive admissions.

Partner with three regional sleep-specialized neurologists to pre-screen referrals according to a standardized checklist and confidence rating (high/uncertain/low), enrolling only high-confidence referrals directly and routing uncertain cases to a single abbreviated in-person visit at the Bonn facility before final acceptance.
levers.review_lever: Rigorous gatekeeping prevents low-yield admissions but delays enrollment; rapid gatekeeping fills beds faster but risks borderline cases consuming facility time and budget. None of the options address how to handle participants who initially meet criteria but fail to produce events despite extended stays, leaving the protocol's response to sustained non-productivity undefined.

levers.name: Night technician cognitive workload and event annotation workflow
levers.consequences: Assigning one technician to monitor eight suites in real time forces rapid sequential event detection and annotation under fatigue and attention-allocation pressure, raising the risk of missed genuine events and annotation errors that cascade into Aim 2 and Aim 3 analysis; rotating three technicians across the 8-suite facility reduces per-technician vigilance burden but increases inter-rater annotation variability and requires redundant training, raising per-night labor cost. The choice directly affects both event-capture sensitivity and the downstream reliability of dual-rater adjudication.
options: Deploy a single rostered night technician per shift, supported by an automated alert system that flags candidate events detected by mattress-sensor thresholds and infrared motion analysis, requiring the technician to review flagged segments in real time and manually annotate confirmed parasomnia behaviors, with all annotations reviewed by the second independent scorer during business hours.

Assign two technicians per shift: one dedicated to 1-4 suites for continuous visual and physiological monitoring with real-time hand-written annotation, and a second staffing suites 5-8 and handling data ingestion and backup procedures, rotating roles weekly to maintain cross-training and reduce monotony-driven missed events.

Institute a hybrid overnight model where a single primary technician monitors all eight suites with live-video wall displays and sensor dashboards, but escalates any flagged or uncertain event to a remote on-call senior sleep physician via secure video call within 60 seconds, who confirms the annotation classification and provides real-time scoring guidance to maintain consistency across nights.
levers.review_lever: Low technician count risks missed events and annotation drift; high technician count inflates salary burden and inter-rater variability. None of the options measure whether alert automation or remote physician oversight actually improves detection sensitivity relative to trained in-person annotation, leaving the trade-off between cost and sensitivity validation unresolved.

levers.name: Control group recruitment cohort size and matching strategy
levers.consequences: A large matched control group (30–40 control nights) provides robust statistical baseline data on false-positive sensor activity and normal arousal-movement patterns, strengthening the specificity benchmarking of Aim 3 semi-automated tools, but diverts recruitment effort and facility time from the primary parasomnia cohort, delaying enrollment of cases and stretching the 3-year operational window. A minimal control arm (5–10 control nights) preserves recruitment focus on parasomnia cases but leaves the event-triage algorithm lacking sufficient negative examples to reliably distinguish parasomnia from normal nocturnal behavior, risking inflated false-positive rates in subsequent deployment.
options: Recruit and house a single control-group cohort of 15 healthy volunteers (no parasomnia or sleep disorder history) for two consecutive nights each at the facility, generating 30 control nights stratified by age and sex to match the incident parasomnia cohort, with scheduling clustered in months 6–9 of year one to concentrate control-group effort and preserve parasomnia enrollment momentum.

Implement an ongoing rolling recruitment of one control participant per month over the full 3-year period, spread across all participant suites as availability permits, generating approximately 36 control nights distributed throughout the operational window and reducing the per-month recruitment pressure compared to parasomnia cases while supporting continuous algorithm validation.

Invite 20–25 unaffected bed partners and household members of enrolled parasomnia participants to complete one night of residential monitoring in the facility under identical sensor and PSG acquisition protocols, treating them as context-matched controls who provide direct behavioral comparison to the parasomnia patient they live with and reduce external recruitment burden.
levers.review_lever: Large control cohorts strengthen algorithm specificity but divert resources from case enrollment; minimal controls accelerate parasomnia enrollment but risk flawed event-triage benchmarking. The options do not specify whether control nights should occur at the same facility (increasing venue competition) or at a separate location, leaving the scheduling and interference model unaddressed.

levers.name: Enhanced-night PSG scheduling cadence and adaptation protocol
levers.consequences: Scheduling enhanced PSG (full scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, leg EMG) on a fixed calendar (night 1, mid-stay night 5–7, night final) ensures uniform data collection and simplifies logistics but may miss the time window when events are most likely to occur if clustering is participant-specific; dynamic scheduling that targets enhanced nights around technician-flagged event patterns or participant-reported event timing concentrates resources on high-yield nights but introduces protocol variance and requires rapid decision-making that strains staffing and equipment turnover.
options: Conduct enhanced-night PSG on days 1, 7, and final night for all participants, maintaining consistent protocol timing regardless of observed event patterns, and reserve any additional PSG escalations only for nights immediately following a technician-flagged candidate event, minimizing scheduling friction and reducing equipment changeovers between suites.

Require the night technician to flag any night with one or more candidate parasomnia events detected via dry-electrode EEG or mattress sensor, triggering scheduling of a full PSG montage for the following night to capture any recurrence at clinical resolution; apply this adaptive escalation policy for all flagged nights regardless of the study timeline, potentially adding 1–3 unplanned enhanced-night acquisitions per participant depending on event frequency.

Implement a hybrid schedule where participants undergo full PSG on nights 1 and final (fixed anchors), but reserve the mid-stay enhanced night for participants who have already triggered at least one technician-flagged event during low-burden monitoring, allowing non-producing participants in weeks 1–2 to skip the mid-stay upgrade and either extend their stay for a later enhanced night or discharge without the third PSG to manage facility capacity.
levers.review_lever: Fixed cadence simplifies logistics but may miss event windows; adaptive escalation captures recurrence but introduces protocol variance and scheduling unpredictability. None of the options specify what happens if a participant produces events on every low-burden night—whether equipment constraints or fatigue limits would then force stay shortening, leaving the upper-bound event-production scenario undefined.

levers.name: Data annotation scoring model and inter-rater reliability target
levers.consequences: Requiring all captured events to be independently scored by two trained raters using AASM criteria before adjudication ensures methodological rigor and produces publishable inter-rater reliability metrics, but doubles the annotation labor cost and extends the timeline from event capture to validated phenotypic data by 4–6 weeks; single-rater annotation (with a second rater spot-checking 10–20% of events) accelerates data finalization and reduces per-event costs but introduces scorer-dependent bias and provides weaker evidence of scoring consistency, undermining Aim 2 publication credibility.
options: Establish a centralized scoring laboratory staffed by two full-time certified sleep technologists or physicians (hired in year two) who review and independently score all captured events within 2 weeks of the raw data becoming available, generating dual scores for 100% of events with inter-rater agreement (Cohen's kappa) reported as a quality metric in all publications.

Conduct real-time annotation during the night shift by the on-duty technician, followed by a single independent review during business hours by a designated senior sleep medicine clinician (the PI or postdoctoral researcher), reserving full dual-rater independent scoring for only 25–30% of events selected by stratified random sampling to provide a reproducibility benchmark while reducing overall annotation burden.

Create a panel-review process where any event flagged by the night technician is presented to a weekly video conference including both postdoctoral researchers, the clinical psychologist, and the PI, with consensus scoring derived by live discussion and recorded voting, treating consensus-derived phenotypic calls as equivalent to independent dual scoring and consolidating annotation expertise.
levers.review_lever: Dual independent scoring ensures rigor but doubles annotation cost and delays data finalization; single-rater with spot-checking accelerates throughput but weakens inter-rater reliability reporting. The options do not specify how scoring disagreement is resolved (majority vote, senior adjudication, data exclusion), leaving the conflict-resolution protocol ambiguous.

levers.name: Geographic recruitment reach and participant travel support model
levers.consequences: Restricting recruitment to the Bonn metropolitan region and nearby Rhineland-North Westphalia (via Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin referral network) reduces screening and coordination overhead and attracts participants with shorter travel distances, but limits the participant pool to local sleep specialists and reduces demographic and clinical diversity, potentially biasing the cohort toward milder or more medically-engaged parasomnia cases. Expanding recruitment nationally or internationally increases participant diversity and case-severity range but multiplies travel logistics, requires enhanced accommodation support, strains bed turnover between longer stays, and introduces language-barrier coordination costs.
options: Limit active recruitment to referrals from University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic and the North Rhine-Westphalia regional network of sleep specialists, offering €80 per-night compensation plus local transportation reimbursement (public transit or modest parking), and allow a maximum of 8-week stays to prioritize local participant throughput and operational simplicity.

Implement a tiered recruitment strategy: primary focus on regional referrals (Bonn and NRW), but explicitly partner with three major sleep medicine centers in Munich, Hamburg, and Berlin, offering each a co-investigator role and a dedicated participant quota in exchange for in-person screening and baseline documentation support that reduces central-site workload.

Solicit international recruitment (EU-wide) through the European Sleep Research Society and AASM, positioning the study as a unique long-term residential research opportunity, provide all non-local participants with a flat €500 travel allowance plus meal coverage, and establish a participant information office in Bonn to handle multilingual consent and logistical coordination for participants traveling >500 km.
levers.review_lever: Regional recruitment limits pool size and diversity but simplifies logistics; national or international recruitment increases case variety but inflates participant support costs and bed-turnover friction. None of the options quantify how travel distance or language differences affect participant adherence to 8-week stays or data-quality consistency, leaving the trade-off between reach and operational friction unmeasured.

levers.name: Publication timeline and preliminary-findings disclosure cadence
levers.consequences: Publishing a single consolidated methods-validation and phenotyping paper after 36 months ensures comprehensive data collection and mature analysis but delays dissemination of early findings to the field and may be perceived as delayed scientific impact; releasing preliminary methods-validation findings at 18–24 months (Aim 1) with phenotyping follow-up at month 36 (Aim 2) accelerates visibility and secures early citations but risks publishing partial datasets that later quality-assurance procedures may necessitate revising, potentially undermining credibility and requiring errata or retraction.
options: Publish a single consolidated methods-validation and longitudinal-characterization manuscript after month 30, integrating all Aim 1 operational data (safety, ethics compliance, attrition) with preliminary Aim 2 phenotyping patterns across the full enrolled cohort, followed by a separate algorithmic-benchmarking paper (Aim 3) in month 36 after the semi-automated tools have been fully developed and tested.

Release a methods-validation and study-protocol paper at month 18 covering recruitment procedures, participant safety outcomes, facility operations, and inter-rater scoring reliability across the first 25–30 participants, allowing early scientific impact and feedback, with a separate longitudinal-phenotyping and algorithm-development paper published at month 36 after the full 50–70 participant cohort data has been finalized.

Commit to monthly preprint postings on medRxiv starting at month 12, releasing preliminary findings on operational metrics, early phenotypic patterns, and algorithm-development progress iteratively, then bundle finalized results into two polished journal submissions (one methods-focused, one clinical-phenotyping-focused) at months 24 and 36 to maximize field visibility and community feedback while maintaining formal publication credibility.
levers.review_lever: Consolidated late publication maximizes data maturity but delays impact; early preliminary publication accelerates visibility but risks incomplete-dataset retractions or errata. None of the options address how to handle secondary findings (e.g., unexpected comorbidity patterns, safety incidents) that may warrant rapid disclosure outside the planned publication timeline, leaving ethical disclosure obligations undefined.

levers.review: Stricter screening delays recruitment but concentrates event yield; relaxed screening accelerates numbers but risks unproductive admissions. levers.consequences: Current plan deposits de-identified physiological data in a recognized repository under data use agreements while restricting bedroom video to on-site access only, balancing open science against privacy constraints and regulatory burden. Open, unrestricted sharing would maximize community access and citation impact but risks privacy-law violations under GDPR and erodes participant confidence in residential monitoring. Closed, institutional-only access preserves privacy but forgoes secondary-research momentum and reuse, limiting the scientific footprint of the 50–70 participant cohort.
options: Deposit de-identified physiological data and event-triage outputs in an international open repository (e.g., OpenNeuro, PhysioNet) with BIDS-standard formatting and minimal data-use-agreement friction, reserving video for institutional-only access through a secure institutional hub; advertise the resource in sleep medicine methods forums to seed secondary-use projects.

Establish a managed-access model via a dedicated institutional repository (University of Bonn's data portal) where external researchers request access via a lightweight application, with data use agreements limited to non-commercial academic use, enabling tracking of reuse while preserving tighter governance than open repositories.

Restrict all physiological data sharing to co-authored collaborative projects initiated by the core team within 3 years post-publication, allowing private-sector partnerships and international collaborations only under formal data-transfer agreements, maximizing control but minimizing secondary-research yield and community benefit.
levers.review: Open sharing maximizes impact but multiplies privacy-review and GDPR-compliance labor; restricted sharing protects privacy but sacrifices scientific reuse and leverage. None of the options explicitly reconcile the tension between open-science funding mandates (increasingly required by DFG grants) and the video-capture sensitivities that make this dataset irreducibly sensitive.

levers.name: Staffing model and shift coverage for night technician roles
levers.consequences: Current plan deploys 3 rotating technicians to cover 8 active suites, providing adequate event-response time and real-time annotation but consuming roughly 60% of personnel budget. Increasing staffing (e.g., 4–5 technicians) improves safety margins and annotation quality but accelerates the cost trajectory and compresses the physical capacity available before a bed-expansion trigger is warranted. Reducing staffing (e.g., 2 technicians with remote backup or daytime-shift annotation catch-up) lowers costs but risks delayed event responses during unusual events and shifts annotation into daytime, potentially introducing scoring fatigue and batch-processing errors.
options: Hire a dedicated night-shift coordinator (in addition to the 3 rotating technicians) to handle real-time event verification, participant welfare checks, and escalation decisions, freeing the technicians for focused annotation and sleep quality, accepting a 15% increase in night-shift personnel cost but improving response times and reducing individual technician cognitive load.

Maintain 3 rotating technicians but introduce a hybrid staffing model where two technicians work the night shift (10pm–6am) for event response and participant safety, and a daytime annotation technician processes flagged-event video during business hours using time-locked physiological playback, reducing night-shift fatigue-related errors.

Reduce staffing to 2 full-time night technicians with an on-call backup technician paid hourly for events flagged as high-probability parasomnia, supplementing night coverage during high-event periods (e.g., weeks 2–4 of stays when event rates often peak), reducing baseline costs while preserving surge capacity.
levers.review: Higher staffing improves response and annotation quality but strains budget; lower staffing cuts costs but risks delayed responses and batch-annotation errors. None of the options address the quality of night-technician decision-making itself—no training or competency assessment schedule is specified for consistent event flagging.

levers.name: Expansion timing and bed-capacity utilization policy
levers.consequences: Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but the plan defers expansion until pilot data (8 suites, months 0–12) demonstrates acceptable data quality, manageable false-alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Early expansion (adding 4 suites by month 8) would accelerate enrollment toward the 50–70 target and reduce per-participant facility overhead, but risks discovering bottlenecks (e.g., annotation backlogs, technician fatigue) only after sunk capital. Delayed expansion (month 18 or later, conditional on metrics) preserves capital flexibility and de-risks staffing scaling, but pushes full-capacity operations into years 2–3 and compresses time for failure-trigger resolution (the month 18 external review threshold).
options: Add 4 suites in parallel with the first 12 months of 8-suite operation, hiring and training additional technicians by month 6 to ensure overlap training with current staff before month 8 activation, accepting earlier capital commitment but shortening the path to 50+ enrollments and spreading setup overhead across a larger bed base.

Conduct a formal readiness review at month 10 (after 8–12 weeks of stable 8-suite operations) assessing data-quality metrics, false-alarm rates, and technician workload; if all metrics are within pre-specified targets, activate 4 additional suites by month 12 with a 4-week technician overlap and training window, deferring major capital until operational proof.

Commit to expand to 12 suites only after the month 18 external review if the program meets enrollment and event-capture benchmarks, conditioning bed expansion on demonstrated operational sustainability and using the interval to refine protocols, reducing early capital risk but squeezing the final 18 months to hit 50–70 enrollments and triggering acceleration pressure.
levers.review: Early expansion scales enrollment faster but risks capital waste and staffing chaos if pilot metrics fail; late expansion preserves flexibility but forces rapid scaling in the final year if month 18 review is positive. All three options assume that adding 4 suites simply scales current staffing ratios linearly—none address the fixed-overhead tail (data engineering, coordination, clinical oversight) that does not scale proportionally with beds.

levers.name: Venue atmosphere and acoustic design trade-off
levers.consequences: A fully soundproofed, clinically appointed suite maximizes technical data isolation but increases perceived surveillance and suppresses naturalistic parasomnia behavior; conversely, preserving domestic acoustics and furnishings encourages behavioral authenticity but introduces environmental noise that degrades signal-to-noise ratio on dry-electrode EEG and complicates technician alertness to genuine events. The balance directly determines whether captured events are representative of the participant's at-home parasomnia phenotype or an artifact of the research environment.
options: Designate two suites as bedroom-faithful (residential wallpaper, conventional lighting, household noise tolerance) for baseline behavioral collection while maintaining full clinical soundproofing and shielding in the remaining six suites for high-fidelity PSG nights, allowing direct phenotypic comparison between naturalistic and controlled settings.

Install modular acoustic panels and dimmable lighting in all eight suites that can be activated only during the three scheduled enhanced-night PSG sessions, keeping the environment domestic by default and minimizing the clinical atmosphere that might suppress parasomnia expression.

Construct a single hybrid demonstration suite designed with interior surfaces that appear residential but incorporate concealed acoustic isolation and electromagnetic shielding, then rotate all participants through this single suite for their enhanced-night PSG acquisition while conducting low-burden monitoring in the remaining seven standard-residential suites.
levers.review: Domestic feel suppresses event capture, while clinical control degrades phenotypic relevance; the options do not address how to measure whether behavior changes between domestic and clinical sub-settings, leaving inter-setting reproducibility unclear.

levers.name: Participant baseline phenotyping depth and recruitment gatekeeping
levers.consequences: Requiring in-depth pre-admission assessment (specialist interview, collateral history, baseline PSG, exclusion adjudication) ensures enrolled participants meet strict parasomnia criteria and reduces unproductive admissions, but extending the enrollment pathway delays first participant arrival and narrows the recruitment funnel, slowing enrollment velocity and increasing per-participant screening costs. Light gatekeeping (self-report plus single clinical phone screen) accelerates enrollment and broadens the recruitment pool but risks admitting borderline candidates whose events are infrequent, misdiagnosed, or not parasomnia-related, inflating unproductive admissions and depleting the contingency budget.
options: Implement a two-stage intake: tier-one screening by sleep clinic telephone interview plus review of any prior PSG to rapidly filter obvious exclusions, with full adjudication and collateral-history collection reserved for tier-one passers, concentrating gatekeeping effort on the 40–50% of applicants who clear the phone screen.

Conduct baseline polysomnography on all applicants at the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic (leveraging existing infrastructure and clinical-care referrals) before enrollment decision, ensuring parasomnia diagnosis is neurophysiologically confirmed and obstructive sleep apnea severity is measured, accepting the 6–8 week pre-enrollment delay but eliminating on-site unproductive admissions.

Partner with three regional sleep-specialized neurologists to pre-screen referrals according to a standardized checklist and confidence rating (high/uncertain/low), enrolling only high-confidence referrals directly and routing uncertain cases to a single abbreviated in-person visit at the Bonn facility before final acceptance.
levers.review: Rigorous gatekeeping prevents low-yield admissions but delays enrollment; rapid gatekeeping fills beds faster but risks borderline cases consuming facility time and budget. None of the options address how to handle participants who initially meet criteria but fail to produce events despite extended stays, leaving the protocol's response to sustained non-productivity undefined.

levers.name: Night technician cognitive workload and event annotation workflow
levers.consequences: Assigning one technician to monitor eight suites in real time forces rapid sequential event detection and annotation under fatigue and attention-allocation pressure, raising the risk of missed genuine events and annotation errors that cascade into Aim 2 and Aim 3 analysis; rotating three technicians across the 8-suite facility reduces per-technician vigilance burden but increases inter-rater annotation variability and requires redundant training, raising per-night labor cost. The choice directly affects both event-capture sensitivity and the downstream reliability of dual-rater adjudication.
options: Deploy a single rostered night technician per shift, supported by an automated alert system that flags candidate events detected by mattress-sensor thresholds and infrared motion analysis, requiring the technician to review flagged segments in real time and manually annotate confirmed parasomnia behaviors, with all annotations reviewed by the second independent scorer during business hours.

Assign two technicians per shift: one dedicated to 1-4 suites for continuous visual and physiological monitoring with real-time hand-written annotation, and a second staffing suites 5-8 and handling data ingestion and backup procedures, rotating roles weekly to maintain cross-training and reduce monotony-driven missed events.

Institute a hybrid overnight model where a single primary technician monitors all eight suites with live-video wall displays and sensor dashboards, but escalates any flagged or uncertain event to a remote on-call senior sleep physician via secure video call within 60 seconds, who confirms the annotation classification and provides real-time scoring guidance to maintain consistency across nights.
levers.review: Low technician count risks missed events and annotation drift; high technician count inflates salary burden and inter-rater variability. None of the options measure whether alert automation or remote physician oversight actually improves detection sensitivity relative to trained in-person annotation, leaving the trade-off between cost and sensitivity validation unresolved.

levers.name: Control group recruitment cohort size and matching strategy
levers.consequences: A large matched control group (30–40 control nights) provides robust statistical baseline data on false-positive sensor activity and normal arousal-movement patterns, strengthening the specificity benchmarking of Aim 3 semi-automated tools, but diverts recruitment effort and facility time from the primary parasomnia cohort, delaying enrollment of cases and stretching the 3-year operational window. A minimal control arm (5–10 control nights) preserves recruitment focus on parasomnia cases but leaves the event-triage algorithm lacking sufficient negative examples to reliably distinguish parasomnia from normal nocturnal behavior, risking inflated false-positive rates in subsequent deployment.
options: Recruit and house a single control-group cohort of 15 healthy volunteers (no parasomnia or sleep disorder history) for two consecutive nights each at the facility, generating 30 control nights stratified by age and sex to match the incident parasomnia cohort, with scheduling clustered in months 6–9 of year one to concentrate control-group effort and preserve parasomnia enrollment momentum.

Implement an ongoing rolling recruitment of one control participant per month over the full 3-year period, spread across all participant suites as availability permits, generating approximately 36 control nights distributed throughout the operational window and reducing the per-month recruitment pressure compared to parasomnia cases while supporting continuous algorithm validation.

Invite 20–25 unaffected bed partners and household members of enrolled parasomnia participants to complete one night of residential monitoring in the facility under identical sensor and PSG acquisition protocols, treating them as context-matched controls who provide direct behavioral comparison to the parasomnia patient they live with and reduce external recruitment burden.
levers.review: Large control cohorts strengthen algorithm specificity but divert resources from case enrollment; minimal controls accelerate parasomnia enrollment but risk flawed event-triage benchmarking. The options do not specify whether control nights should occur at the same facility (increasing venue competition) or at a separate location, leaving the scheduling and interference model unaddressed.

levers.name: Enhanced-night PSG scheduling cadence and adaptation protocol
levers.consequences: Scheduling enhanced PSG (full scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, leg EMG) on a fixed calendar (night 1, mid-stay night 5–7, night final) ensures uniform data collection and simplifies logistics but may miss the time window when events are most likely to occur if clustering is participant-specific; dynamic scheduling that targets enhanced nights around technician-flagged event patterns or participant-reported event timing concentrates resources on high-yield nights but introduces protocol variance and requires rapid decision-making that strains staffing and equipment turnover.
options: Conduct enhanced-night PSG on days 1, 7, and final night for all participants, maintaining consistent protocol timing regardless of observed event patterns, and reserve any additional PSG escalations only for nights immediately following a technician-flagged candidate event, minimizing scheduling friction and reducing equipment changeovers between suites.

Require the night technician to flag any night with one or more candidate parasomnia events detected via dry-electrode EEG or mattress sensor, triggering scheduling of a full PSG montage for the following night to capture any recurrence at clinical resolution; apply this adaptive escalation policy for all flagged nights regardless of the study timeline, potentially adding 1–3 unplanned enhanced-night acquisitions per participant depending on event frequency.

Implement a hybrid schedule where participants undergo full PSG on nights 1 and final (fixed anchors), but reserve the mid-stay enhanced night for participants who have already triggered at least one technician-flagged event during low-burden monitoring, allowing non-producing participants in weeks 1–2 to skip the mid-stay upgrade and either extend their stay for a later enhanced night or discharge without the third PSG to manage facility capacity.
levers.review: Fixed cadence simplifies logistics but may miss event windows; adaptive escalation captures recurrence but introduces protocol variance and scheduling unpredictability. None of the options specify what happens if a participant produces events on every low-burden night—whether equipment constraints or fatigue limits would then force stay shortening, leaving the upper-bound event-production scenario undefined.

levers.name: Data annotation scoring model and inter-rater reliability target
levers.consequences: Requiring all captured events to be independently scored by two trained raters using AASM criteria before adjudication ensures methodological rigor and produces publishable inter-rater reliability metrics, but doubles the annotation labor cost and extends the timeline from event capture to validated phenotypic data by 4–6 weeks; single-rater annotation (with a second rater spot-checking 10–20% of events) accelerates data finalization and reduces per-event costs but introduces scorer-dependent bias and provides weaker evidence of scoring consistency, undermining Aim 2 publication credibility.
options: Establish a centralized scoring laboratory staffed by two full-time certified sleep technologists or physicians (hired in year two) who review and independently score all captured events within 2 weeks of the raw data becoming available, generating dual scores for 100% of events with inter-rater agreement (Cohen's kappa) reported as a quality metric in all publications.

Conduct real-time annotation during the night shift by the on-duty technician, followed by a single independent review during business hours by a designated senior sleep medicine clinician (the PI or postdoctoral researcher), reserving full dual-rater independent scoring for only 25–30% of events selected by stratified random sampling to provide a reproducibility benchmark while reducing overall annotation burden.

Create a panel-review process where any event flagged by the night technician is presented to a weekly video conference including both postdoctoral researchers, the clinical psychologist, and the PI, with consensus scoring derived by live discussion and recorded voting, treating consensus-derived phenotypic calls as equivalent to independent dual scoring and consolidating annotation expertise.
levers.review: Dual independent scoring ensures rigor but doubles annotation cost and delays data finalization; single-rater with spot-checking accelerates throughput but weakens inter-rater reliability reporting. The options do not specify how scoring disagreement is resolved (majority vote, senior adjudication, data exclusion), leaving the conflict-resolution protocol ambiguous.

levers.name: Geographic recruitment reach and participant travel support model
levers.consequences: Restricting recruitment to the Bonn metropolitan region and nearby Rhineland-North Westphalia (via Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin referral network) reduces screening and coordination overhead and attracts participants with shorter travel distances, but limits the participant pool to local sleep specialists and reduces demographic and clinical diversity, potentially biasing the cohort toward milder or more medically-engaged parasomnia cases. Expanding recruitment nationally or internationally increases participant diversity and case-severity range but multiplies travel logistics, requires enhanced accommodation support, strains bed turnover between longer stays, and introduces language-barrier coordination costs.
options: Limit active recruitment to referrals from University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic and the North Rhine-Westphalia regional network of sleep specialists, offering €80 per-night compensation plus local transportation reimbursement (public transit or modest parking), and allow a maximum of 8-week stays to prioritize local participant throughput and operational simplicity.

Implement a tiered recruitment strategy: primary focus on regional referrals (Bonn and NRW), but explicitly partner with three major sleep medicine centers in Munich, Hamburg, and Berlin, offering each a co-investigator role and a dedicated participant quota in exchange for in-person screening and baseline documentation support that reduces central-site workload.

Solicit international recruitment (EU-wide) through the European Sleep Research Society and AASM, positioning the study as a unique long-term residential research opportunity, provide all non-local participants with a flat €500 travel allowance plus meal coverage, and establish a participant information office in Bonn to handle multilingual consent and logistical coordination for participants traveling >500 km.
levers.review: Regional recruitment limits pool size and diversity but simplifies logistics; national or international recruitment increases case variety but inflates participant support costs and bed-turnover friction. None of the options quantify how travel distance or language differences affect participant adherence to 8-week stays or data-quality consistency, leaving the trade-off between reach and operational friction unmeasured.

levers.name: Publication timeline and preliminary-findings disclosure cadence
levers.consequences: Publishing a single consolidated methods-validation and phenotyping paper after 36 months ensures comprehensive data collection and mature analysis but delays dissemination of early findings to the field and may be perceived as delayed scientific impact; releasing preliminary methods-validation findings at 18–24 months (Aim 1) with phenotyping follow-up at month 36 (Aim 2) accelerates visibility and secures early citations but risks publishing partial datasets that later quality-assurance procedures may necessitate revising, potentially undermining credibility and requiring errata or retraction.
options: Publish a single consolidated methods-validation and longitudinal-characterization manuscript after month 30, integrating all Aim 1 operational data (safety, ethics compliance, attrition) with preliminary Aim 2 phenotyping patterns across the full enrolled cohort, followed by a separate algorithmic-benchmarking paper (Aim 3) in month 36 after the semi-automated tools have been fully developed and tested.

Release a methods-validation and study-protocol paper at month 18 covering recruitment procedures, participant safety outcomes, facility operations, and inter-rater scoring reliability across the first 25–30 participants, allowing early scientific impact and feedback, with a separate longitudinal-phenotyping and algorithm-development paper published at month 36 after the full 50–70 participant cohort data has been finalized.

Commit to monthly preprint postings on medRxiv starting at month 12, releasing preliminary findings on operational metrics, early phenotypic patterns, and algorithm-development progress iteratively, then bundle finalized results into two polished journal submissions (one methods-focused, one clinical-phenotyping-focused) at months 24 and 36 to maximize field visibility and community feedback while maintaining formal publication credibility.
levers.review: Consolidated late publication maximizes data maturity but delays impact; early preliminary publication accelerates visibility but risks incomplete-dataset retractions or errata. None of the options address how to handle secondary findings (e.g., unexpected comorbidity patterns, safety incidents) that may warrant rapid disclosure outside the planned publication timeline, leaving ethical disclosure obligations undefined.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — identify the primary trade-off this lever introduces, then state the specific gap the three options leave unaddressed.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season adds a fixed cost that erases the per-unit savings unless utilization reaches year-round levels."
     - "Each additional clinical site requires its own IRB approval, site-initiation visit, and staff credentialing — a sequential overhead that compounds rather than parallelizes, so doubling site count does not halve enrollment time."
     - "Pooling catastrophe risk across three coastal regions reduces expected annual loss on paper, but a single regional hurricane season can correlate all three simultaneously, turning the diversification assumption into a concentration risk at the worst possible moment."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Length Limits**
   - Keep each `review_lever` to one sentence (20–40 words). State the trade-off and the gap concisely.
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.

File 'purpose.md':
**Purpose:** business

**Purpose Detailed:** Research facility establishment, data collection, and analysis for scientific publication and grant funding.

**Topic:** Establishment of a residential research unit for the study of adult NREM parasomnias

File 'plan_type.md':
This plan requires one or more physical locations. It cannot be executed digitally.

**Explanation:** This plan *unequivocally requires* a physical location (the research unit in Bonn, Germany), physical renovations, physical equipment, in-person staffing, and physical participants. The entire research project *revolves around* physical data collection and analysis in a real-world setting. The plan also requires physical travel to the location.
